name: Bytes.Decode.Branchable

unions.name: Decoder
unions.comment: A decoder for a certain type of value.

values.name: andThen
values.comment:
Decode one thing, and then another thing based on the first thing.

This is very useful to make the content of your data drive your decoder. As an
example, consider a string encoded as the length of the string, followed by the
actual data:

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D exposing (Decoder)

    string : Decoder String
    string =
        D.unsignedInt8
            |> D.andThen D.string

    [ E.unsignedInt8 5
    , E.string "hello"
    ]
        |> E.sequence
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode string
    --> Just "hello"
values.type: (a -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder b) -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder a -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder b

values.name: bytes
values.comment: Parse `count` bytes as `Bytes`.

values.name: decode
values.comment:
Run the given decoder on the provided bytes.

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D

    E.string "hello"
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode (D.string 5)
    --> Just "hello"

    E.string "hello"
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode (D.string 6)
    --> Nothing

values.name: fail
values.comment:
A Decoder that always fails.

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D

    E.sequence []
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode D.fail
    --> Nothing

values.name: float32
values.comment: Decode 4 bytes into a Float.

values.name: float64
values.comment: Decode 8 bytes into a Float.

values.name: ignore
values.comment:
Ignore the value produced by a decoder.

Note that the decoder must still succeed for the pipeline to succeed. This means
you can use this for checking the value of something, without using the value.

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D exposing (Decoder)

    match : Int -> Decoder Int
    match expected =
        D.unsignedInt8
            |> D.andThen
                (\actual ->
                    if expected == actual then
                        D.succeed actual
                    else
                        D.fail
                )

    decoder : Decoder ()
    decoder =
        D.succeed ()
            |> D.ignore (match 66)

    E.unsignedInt8 66
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode decoder
    --> Just ()

    E.unsignedInt8 44
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode decoder
    --> Nothing
values.type: Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder ignore -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder keep -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder keep

values.name: keep
values.comment:
Keep the value produced by a decoder in a pipeline.

Together with [`succeed`](#succeed) and [`ignore`](#ignore), this allows writing
pretty flexible decoders in a straightforward manner: the order in which things
are parsed is apparent.

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D exposing (Decoder)

    decoder : Decoder (Int, Int)
    decoder =
        D.succeed Tuple.pair
            |> D.keep D.unsignedInt8
            |> D.ignore D.unsignedInt8
            |> D.keep D.unsignedInt8

    [ E.unsignedInt8 12
    , E.unsignedInt8 3
    , E.unsignedInt8 45
    ]
        |> E.sequence
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode decoder
    --> Just ( 12, 45 )
values.type: Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder a -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder (a -> b) -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder b

values.name: loop
values.comment:
Loop a decoder until it declares it is done looping.

The `Step` type in the signature comes from the `Bytes.Decode` module in elm/bytes.
Here is how `repeat` is defined for example.

    import Bytes.Decode
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D exposing (Decoder)

    repeat : Decoder value -> Int -> Decoder (List value)
    repeat p count =
        D.loop ( count, [] ) (repeatHelp p)

    repeatHelp p ( count, acc ) =
        if count <= 0 then
            succeed (Bytes.Decode.Done (List.reverse acc))

        else
            map (\v -> Bytes.Decode.Loop ( count - 1, v :: acc )) p
values.type: state -> (state -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder (Bytes.Decode.Step state a)) -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder a

values.name: map
values.comment:
Transform the value a decoder produces

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D

    E.string "hello"
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode (D.map String.length (D.string 5))
    --> Just 5

values.name: map2
values.comment:
Combine two decoders into a single one.

    import Bytes exposing (Bytes)
    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D exposing (Decoder)

    input : Bytes
    input =
        [ E.unsignedInt8 2
        , E.string "wat"
        ]
            |> E.sequence
            |> E.encode

    map2Example : Decoder String
    map2Example =
        D.map2 String.repeat D.unsignedInt8 (D.string 3)

    D.decode map2Example input
    --> Just "watwat"

Note that the effect of `map2` (and, in fact, every `map` variation) can also be
achieved using a combination of [`succeed`](#succeed) and [`keep`](#keep).

    equivalent : Decoder String
    equivalent =
        D.succeed String.repeat
            |> D.keep D.unsignedInt8
            |> D.keep (D.string 3)

    D.decode equivalent input
    --> Just "watwat"
values.type: (x -> y -> z) -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder x -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder y -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder z

values.name: map3
values.comment: Combine 3 decoders into a single one.
values.type: (w -> x -> y -> z) -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder w -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder x -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder y -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder z

values.name: map4
values.comment: Combine 4 decoders into a single one.
values.type: (v -> w -> x -> y -> z) -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder v -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder w -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder x -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder y -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder z

values.name: map5
values.comment: Combine 5 decoders into a single one.
values.type: (u -> v -> w -> x -> y -> z) -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder u -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder v -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder w -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder x -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder y -> Bytes.Decode.Branchable.Decoder z

values.name: oneOf
values.comment:
Tries a bunch of decoders and succeeds with the first one to succeed.

All decoder alternatives start at the same location in the bytes.

values.name: repeat
values.comment:
Repeat a given decoder `count` times.

The order of arguments is based on the common occurence of reading the number of
times to repeat something through a decoder.

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D exposing (Decoder)

    intList : Decoder (List Int)
    intList =
        D.unsignedInt8
            |> D.andThen (D.repeat D.unsignedInt8)

    [ 5, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 ]
        |> List.map E.unsignedInt8
        |> E.sequence
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode intList
    --> Just [ 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 ]

values.name: signedInt16
values.comment: Decode two bytes into an integer from -32768 to 32767.

values.name: signedInt32
values.comment: Decode four bytes into an integer from -2147483648 to 2147483647.

values.name: signedInt8
values.comment: Decode one byte into an integer from -128 to 127.

values.name: skip
values.comment:
Skip a number of bytes in a pipeline.

This is similar to `ignore`, but rather than decoding a value and discarding it,
this just goes ahead and skips them altogether.

values.name: string
values.comment:
Decode `count` bytes representing UTF-8 characters into a String.

Note that Elm strings use UTF-16. As a result, the `String.length` will not
always agree with the number of bytes that went into it!

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D

    [ 0xF0, 0x9F, 0x91, 0x8D ]
        |> List.map E.unsignedInt8
        |> E.sequence
        |> E.encode
        |> D.decode (D.string 4)
    --> Just "👍"

values.name: succeed
values.comment:
Always succeed with the given value.

    import Bytes.Encode as E
    import Bytes.Decode.Branchable as D

    E.encode (E.sequence [])
        |> D.decode (D.succeed "hi there")
    --> Just "hi there"

values.name: unsignedInt16
values.comment: Decode two bytes into an integer from 0 to 65535.

values.name: unsignedInt32
values.comment: Decode four bytes into an integer from 0 to 4294967295.

values.name: unsignedInt8
values.comment: Decode one byte into an integer from 0 to 255.
